titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Gold Rush Collection
titlestmt.author: by Alison Clemens

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday October 26, 2018 at 04:47

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: This collection encompasses accessions of single letters and small groups of letters and other manuscripts, pertaining to the California Gold Rush, which the library has chosen to group and list as an artificial collection. Letters discuss voyages to the Pacific coast, including journeys through Panama; mining life in California; the inhabitants of California, including Mormons; vigilance committees in San Francisco; and the establishment of a police force in Nevada City, California. Some letters include pictorial letterheads.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Collection of documents acquired by gift and purchase from various sources. Source information is recorded in the finding aid or on the folders. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Gold Rush Collection is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Gold Rush Collection. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection encompasses accessions of single letters and small groups of letters and other manuscripts, pertaining to the California Gold Rush, which the library has chosen to group and list as an artificial collection. Letters discuss voyages to the Pacific coast, including journeys through Panama; mining life in California; the inhabitants of California, including Mormons; vigilance committees in San Francisco; and the establishment of a police force in Nevada City, California.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The collection is arranged alphabetically by creator.

subject.part: Indians of North America -- Religion -- 19th century.

geogname.part: California -- Social life and customs -- 19th century

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from A. C. Edmunds from Downieville, California to his family in Michigan concerning his life in California, the Universalist faith, his work as a missionary preacher to Gold Rush miners, and the temperance movement. With three letters to Cynthia Edmunds from unidentified friends, a poem entitled "My Sister's Grave," and unidentified manuscript poems and speeches.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from Sacramento, California, to Field's sister, commenting on raising hogs during the Gold Rush and the high level of immigration to California.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from San Francisco to a "Mr. Merriman." The letters concern Field's efforts to sell newspapers, become a merchant, and work as a miner. Field gives lengthy descriptions of the process of mining and the life of miners.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from Mariposa, California to Fox's wife Ida, and a letter to Fox from an E. Leland regarding Leland's employment.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typescript copy of a letter from San Francisco to "Mr. and Mrs. Henry Conklin" regarding Hammond's journey to California, especially his travels through Yosemite.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from San Francisco concerning Hyde's financial and legal struggles, including his efforts to obtain money he is owed by selling Los Angeles gold stock.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Seventeen autograph letters, signed, to Jones' father from a steamship, Crescent City, California, and Smith River Valley, California. Letters concern mining, lumber, an 1860 massacre at Carson Valley of white people by Native Americans, and Indian rain dances.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, to his brother-in-law, Benjamin Moors Stanley, describing his life as a miner in Diamond Springs, California, particularly his mining work and social interactions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, to James and Hetty Ann Kerr from members of the Kerr-Johnston family. Letters describe the Gold Rush, Fort Laramie, Fort Kearney, crossing the plains to California, and mining life.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph statement, signed, by Latimer as U.S. Attorney, District of California, San Francisco, that no indictment has been found against any persons for collecting the Foreign Miners License Tax.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from Peru and Sacramento, Sonora, and San Francisco, California to Leibee's wife and other family members describing mining and life in California, his fellow miners, moving to Peru to mine, religious matters, and the American Civil War.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, to Lewis from an unidentified writer from San Francisco regarding a book order and mining.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Stockton regarding a local precinct election, with a small sketch of the San Joaquin River and a report of a journey passing the river.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from the Steamer Panama, a letter to Lott from an unidentified friend, a 1867 circular on mining claims, a land indenture on Feather River, and an IOU.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from New York regarding establishing a household in San Francisco and obtaining enough capital to do so.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from San Francisco to a friend about life in California.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Marcy, U.S. Secretary of War, from Washington, D.C. regarding reports of gold in California.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Marysville, California to "Maggie" on economic conditions, getting his photograph taken in a "Photographie Saloon," and attempting to convince "Maggie" to come to California.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, to Merrow's sister regarding his life in California and the vigilance movement in San Francisco.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Bristol, Rhode Island to William Bonney, a sailor, regarding gold mania.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from Rio de Janeiro and San Francisco to Morrison's family regarding his stay in Rio de Janeiro and his voyage to California via Cape Horn, as well as the population explosion and high cost of living in San Francisco.

did.unittitle: The People of Nevada City to the County Court of Yuba County

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph petition to establish a police force, written in an unidentified hand and signed by eleven men.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from San Francisco and Greenwood Ranch, California to Pond's parents regarding mob law, violence, and vigilance committees during the Gold Rush, as well as family matters and expenses.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from Sacramento to Scranton's brother regarding gold, crops, "yellow boys," and a flood.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from Georgetown, California concerning Shumway's work in the gold mines.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letters, signed, from Sacramento regarding the brothers' business supplying goods to miners, including milk, and Lawson's 1852 death by bilious colic or cholera.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, to James Guthrie, Secretary of the Treasury, Custom House, San Francisco, regarding Smith's work and pay as a clerk in the Custom House

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from San Francisco about the lumber trade and commerce.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Sacramento about trying to make his fortune in gold mining.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from San Francisco to a "Captain I. Snow" about Thorndike's passage to California and arrival in San Francisco.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph letter, signed, from Benicia, California requesting to send $250 home in gold dust.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters from unidentified authors from San Francisco, Panama, New York, and Peoria on a variety of subjects, including mining, a voyage through Panama from New York to San Francisco, a description of the city of San Francisco, and Mormons. Also includes a letter on Levi pictorial letterhead advertising denim pants to miners.
